does: This survey systematically catalogs security threats unique to agentic AI systems—autonomous agents built on LLMs with planning, memory, and tool-use capabilities. It develops a taxonomy of vulnerabilities specific to agents operating across web, software, and physical environments, distinguishing these risks from traditional AI safety and conventional software security concerns. The paper reviews existing benchmarks and evaluation methodologies for measuring agent security, catalogs defense strategies spanning technical approaches and governance frameworks, and identifies critical open challenges in building secure-by-design agent systems. It synthesizes fragmented current research into a coherent landscape.
connects: This work bridges two previously separate research threads: LLM safety and autonomous systems security. It extends prior work on AI safety risks (such as goal misalignment and prompt injection) into the new domain of autonomous agents with real-world environmental interaction. The paper builds on existing LLM security research addressing jailbreaks and adversarial inputs, while incorporating insights from classical software security and robotics safety literature. It responds to growing concerns about agents' amplified attack surface—their ability to chain multiple tool invocations, persist state, and act independently creates novel failure modes beyond individual model outputs. The taxonomy synthesizes threats identified across recent agent-specific research and benchmark papers.
enables: This framework enables several downstream research directions: development of agent-specific threat models and security requirements specifications; creation of more comprehensive evaluation benchmarks tailored to agentic behaviors rather than static model outputs; design of defense mechanisms that operate at the agent architecture level rather than just the LLM layer; and integration of security considerations into agent development practices from inception. The taxonomy and open challenges identified can guide standardization efforts for secure agent deployment, support development of formal verification methods for agent plans, and motivate research into interpretability techniques for understanding agent decision-making under adversarial conditions. The governance perspectives discussed could inform emerging policy and certification frameworks for autonomous systems.
